sentences.text: No.
We knew nothing.
We were never told anything till we got to the place in question.
We never knew a thing.
We didn't know what to think.
We didn't know what to think.
First, we thought maybe we'd see the men and the children.
But of course, that was an illusion.
Nothing at all.
Nothing at all.
Not as far as we knew.
We never knew any that lived-- that were Nazis before the war.
Where we lived, we never saw anything, or in the shops or anything.
No, nothing.
Yes, when we left.
Yes, but we never knew anything who was and who wasn't.
Right, right.
Why they shifted us from where we lived to the other place in Amsterdam?
Oh.
Had nowhere to go.
My sister was there before I was, with my nephew, and we're staying with friends.
And I stayed with school friends, non-Jewish anti-fascist.
Oh, I'm sorry.
Yes.
From the salt mine, they took us to Hamburg.
For eight days.
Back-- from Hamburg, they took us back in the trucks.
Didn't tell us.
We arrived in Copenhagen.
We still didn't know.
We saw the Red Cross, so we knew that we-- up to a point-- we were free.
But we were too ill.
By that-- if it had gone on another month, I would never have survived.
I was about 5 and 1/2 stone, just able to move.
Well, I knew we were free.
And we did what we were told.
We-- we were taken to a big school that had been converted into a hospital, and then we knew we were safe.
Yes.
It was before the war ended.
It was on the-- I believe-- the 4th or-- 4th or 5th of May, and the war ended on the 8th.
So we were free before that.
I was in the hospital for six months, nearly six months, till August.
I forget the date.
But I'll tell you what happened.
While we were in the hospital, the Jewish people living in Malmo never came to the hospital, not once.
We were all mixed then, non-Jewish and Jew.
Never came.
But a priest used to come round every morning.
So us English girls decided we were going to ask him if he would go to the British Embassy and see if he could get some letters that we could send home.
He-- we asked him, and he said, of course.
And he went, and he came back.
And he said, the airfields have not been freed yet-- you know, for flying-- but as soon as they are, I'll go and get them for you.
Which he did.
So I wrote an airmail letter [SOBBING] to my mother, not knowing what had happened to her.
But my sister got the letter, and she answered it.
And my brother was home on-- she answered it, and he was home on compassionate leave anyway.
No, that-- I'm telling it wrong.
The first Red Cross letter was from Westerbork, and Mother knew where we were.
My mother was killed.
And next door us lived a schoolmaster, and he saw the postman.
The postman said, I've got this letter, and I can't get an answer.
He said, give it to me, I know where the son lived.
He lived quite near my mother, you see, married.
And he took it, and my brother knew where I was.
So I got a Red Cross letter from him in the hospital in Sweden.
And he wrote and said that he was thankful that-- where I was, and would I [SOBBING] write and tell him what happened.
Because I'd written I was by myself, and what happened to the children and my late husband.
So I went back and told him.
So he got in touch with the Red Cross, and the Red Cross got in touch with Belsen, and they told him that my children, my daughter-- God rest her soul-- and my son were alive in Belsen.
So he made arrangements for the children to go to England, but-- and wrote to me to tell me that the children were alive.
Right?
So of course, I was thankful.
So my brother in England sent me some money.
So the first thing I did when I was better, later-- much later-- got something for him to wear, something for my daughter-- God rest-- to wear.
But anyway, that was later.
So he wrote and told me that he was getting the children over.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
I had a letter from my uncle to tell my dad's youngest brother to-- to say that they were thankful I was alive, but hopes that I'll be strong enough to bear what I still might have to bear.
And I didn't understand that for one minute, knowing my children were all right.
I thought my husband was OK.
[SIGHS]
So then, I got a letter when I was on the road to recovery-- that's why I was in the hospital for three months-- to say that my Helen, God rest her soul, had died.
So that put me back in hospital another couple of weeks, you can imagine.
Did they tell you how?
No, never told me how.
And-- and then, when we were getting better, we were transferred from Malmo to Gothenburg.
And we were all in little chalets there to recuperate.
And there, we were fed and everything.
But when we were first in hospital, all we had was milk and water, because they'd learned from prisoners that had been there before, to feed them was to kill them.
Because the-- the Danish people, when they learned all these women were there, they used to throw food over, and the doctors stopped them from doing it.
But then, they were so kind.
They used to send us over a comb, and a brush, and lipstick, things like that.
We did appreciate.
And the Swedish government gave us one krona every week.
So that was--
When we were getting better.
Jewish people didn't come.
That, I can't say.
That, I do not know.
The parson came, as I told you.
No rabbi, nobody.
But I couldn't understand that, because they must have known that there were Jewish prisoners somewhere, that we were there.
No, nobody came.
Maybe they didn't want to know they were Jewish.
That's possible.
I've always made that excuse, hoping it's true, that they didn't know.
Never, ever met a Jewish person.
Never.
We were allowed to go for a walk, you know.
Never met a person.
Never.
I didn't find that out till-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, I'll tell you what happened.
I didn't find that out till I back working again in Holland.
That was a long time after, because, first, we went to England.
And I stayed and my sister stayed with friends, and I stayed with my sister-in-law in Holland, my eldest brother's wife, in London.
And I said to my sister Anne-- I was there for two weeks-- and I said, Anne, I'm not staying.
I said, I'm not staying.
I said, I'm going to write to the family solicitor in Holland and ask him if he can get us a flat, get me a flat.
Till my-- my brother was looking for a flat for me in England, and for my sister.
But the people that had been bombed had priority.
So I said to Anne, my sister-- God rest her soul-- Anne, I'm writing to a solicitor, a Jewish solicitor-- Mr.
Levy, he went to Palestine later-- that-- or Israel, I should say, not Palestine-- went to Palestine long after the war.
I wrote and said, could you possibly get me a flat in the area near where I lived before?
Which he did, and he wrote back.
And I said to Anne, my sister, Anne.
She said, I'd like to come.
I said, right.
So she came back with me with my little nephew.
So we lived there.
Then we had to go back and forth to The Hague to get our British passports.
That took us till 1946 to get our passport.
Let's clarify something.
You went from Sweden.
Where did you go from Sweden?
Oh, from Sweden, they said that the mothers who know they have children alive are first to go.
So they took us to Stockholm.
We stayed in a hotel in Stockholm for a week, and then they flew us back to Holland, to Eindhoven.
And from there, back to-- I went to Amsterdam and stayed with a school friend of my son's parents, who lived in the same block, non-Jewish.
Over.
We went to England after we got our passports.
That was in 1946.
That's when I stayed with-- and my brother-- but I was working then.
Because I said to my sister-- God rest her soul-- I can't sit here every day and not do anything.
And there was an advert in the paper for a millinery designer.
So I said, I'm going after this.
She said, you're crazy.
You haven't done it for 17 years.
I said, look, Anne, I'm going to try.
So I went for an interview.
And he was a Jewish man.
I think he was German.
I'm not sure, because I met him and his wife later.
And I told him exactly that I hadn't done it for 17 years, and I was willing to try.
So he thought about it, and then he said, all right.
He said, I'll give you a trial for a month.
You-- I have to have a new collection in a month.
If you do it, I'll get you whatever you need.
If it sells, you're in.
If it doesn't sell, we'll part good friends.
And I did it.
Can you imagine the sleepless nights I had?
Not knowing what they were wearing or anything.
So I used to walk round the shops in Holland to have a look, give me an idea of what they were wearing.
So I went, and I worked for a month.
Tell me, how did you find out that your husband was not alive?
That I-- about my mother?
About my husband?
I never found that out.
A long, long time.
When I was working in Holland, I saw him.
Because I thought if my-- to get in touch with the Red Cross, and get to know what [SOBBING] happened to my daughter, and if he could find out what happened to my husband.
So he did this.
And I was working, and he phoned me up one day, and he said, Sis-- he used to call me Sis-- Sis, he says, I've got news for you from the Red Cross.
I want you to come to the office.
So I said, well, I can't leave, because, all these work people and girls I had under me, I can't leave till 5:00.
Doesn't matter, I'll wait for you.
Which he did.
And I went to his office, and he gave me the Red Cross letter.
And it said that my daughter was buried-- died in Belsen and that my husband was in a concentration camp.
That my daughter-- God rest her soul-- died after the liberation, and that my husband was-- I don't know if this is true-- but there were five of the workers left, and they got shot.
That was that.
So then I thought, my daughter's buried in Belsen.
Maybe-- I didn't know it was a mass grave, you see-- maybe I could get her body transferred to England and have her buried where my parents are buried, in a Jewish cemetery.
Because I've got the pictures of them in a Jewish cemetery.
So he wrote again, and they said she was buried in a mass grave, which isn't true.
And my husband was shot, so probably also.
But-- so I couldn't do anything about that.
But Maurice has found out that, as she died after the liberation, she's buried in a grave with only those children that died there.
So he's going to do something about that.
I want him to do something about that, which I know my son-- God bless him-- will do.
Maurice?
I didn't find Maurice till I got to England.
He-- my brother flew him out.
My brother sent him to a private school where his son was going, and he was with them for about three months.
Then my sister-in-law said-- she's in England-- she couldn't look after two boys.
So my cousin-- who was the son of my-- my dad's youngest brother-- took him, and they lived in Kenton in Middlesex, in England.
And he sent Maurice to school and then kept him there.
They are now in Australia.
They lived-- they lived in South Africa afterwards.
And now they're in Australia, and we're all-- I'm still in touch.
I found-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I found out how my daughter died, I think, through my son.
Yeah, I think that was it.
[INTERPOSING VOICES] Because the-- the-- the Red Cross letter I had from my brother when I was in the hospital just said that she died in Belsen after the war.
He was with her all the time.
They-- they-- there was-- I don't know exactly how many children alive, because my son has met them all now.
And they were all together.

And this nurse-- Polish nurse that's just been honored by the Dutch queen and also here, I believe-- looked after the-- she heard children crying, and she went to see where they were, and it was these children that were over.
No, but my-- I-- my son knows the nurse, yes.
The name, because he's met her now.
Typhus.
They all-- all the children had typhus.
My son also had typhus.
I was united with my son when we got our British passports and we went to England the first time.
That was--  [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] see?
I forgot.
It's before I went back to Holland.
I-- I don't--
Maurice-- Maurice will give you the date, yeah.
With my son?
My son.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I met my son in England before I went permanently back to-- to London, to England.
Because while I was working, I-- that was afterwards-- I-- I used to go, like, for a weekend and see him.
But I'd see-- I met him before that.
You can imagine.
I can't describe it.
[SOBBING] I can't.
Because he'd already left Holland when I got to Holland in the first instant.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
In the first place, I took him back.
In the first instance, I took him back with me, and he didn't want to go.
And I thought, well, I'm not leaving him now I've found him.
So he had a bicycle in Holland for one of his birthdays, with those wooden blocks, because he couldn't reach the pedals.
So I thought, well, the only way, maybe, I'll get him to go with me is to buy him a new bicycle in London.
So I bought him a Raleigh bicycle-- I have to tell you this, because it relates to later on-- and he still didn't want to go, but he did come back with me.
And when we got back, the Diamond Group knew that we were back.
And because his father was so well liked, and everything, the Diamond Group decided they would take him as an apprentice, which is a three-year apprenticeship.
They-- you have to pay for that, but no money from me would they take-- I didn't have it in the first place-- would they take, because of what happened.
So he went to work in the factory.
He hated it.
Then my brother, who had been a prisoner of war in Japan, came over with a friend to see how we were getting on-- my sister was back with me in Holland at that time-- how we were getting on.
And he said to me, Sis, he said, I'm going to take Maurice back with me.
So I said, why?
He said, because you'll lose him if the child can't stomach it here.
Because if I went with my sister and her son on a tram, while the four of us were there, they used-- both the boys used to turn round to see who was behind them.
They were scared.
So he said, I'm taking him back.
I said, Michael, I'm not letting him go till I can go, till Sam finds me a-- a flat.
So anyway, cut a long story short, he went out.
I said, I'm not-- he went out and sold his bicycle and said, I'm going.
So I had to let him go.
How old was he then?
He was 14, I think, about 14.
Yeah, must have been 14.
Pardon?
When he was 14?
Well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he was born in '32.
It must be--
Yeah, must be '46.
Am I drawing it out too long?
No.
Yeah.
Finally, my brother got me a flat in-- near-- in North London, near where I used to live, in a-- a-- there were four in the block, and I had the downstairs one.
There was a back garden and a little front garden, so it was nice.
And I used to have to travel to the West End to go to work.
I worked in England, yeah.
I remarried at the end of 1950.
I met him-- I was invited to a dinner party, and he was there, and he was a widower.
He'd lost his wife, had no children.
But I didn't think anything at all about it, nothing at all.
I didn't-- had no thoughts of getting married.
